flashcards.back: The practice of writing programs that interact directly with the Unix OS using system calls, process management, IPC mechanisms, and file I/O operations to leverage kernel services for creating efficient, robust, and portable applications.

A programmatic interface through which a user process requests a service from the Unix kernel. It is the fundamental interface between user space and kernel space, providing controlled access to privileged operations like file handling, process control, and memory management.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Kernel Space and User Space?
flashcards.back: Kernel Space is the privileged mode where the OS kernel executes with full access to hardware and memory. User Space is where user applications run with restricted privileges, isolated from hardware access. Communication between them occurs via system calls.

Core principles: (1) Write programs that do one thing and do it well, (2) Write programs to work together, (3) Write programs to handle text streams as it's the universal interface. Emphasizes simplicity, modularity, and composability.

Portable Operating System Interface - a family of standards specified by IEEE to ensure portability across Unix-like systems. Defines API, command-line shells, and utility interfaces. POSIX compliance ensures code runs on different Unix variants.

flashcards.front: What is the role of the C Library in Unix System Programming?
flashcards.back: The C library (libc) provides wrapper functions around system calls, converting them into standard C function calls. It handles error checking, sets errno, and provides a user-friendly API while bridging to kernel system call interfaces.

flashcards.front: How does an API differ from a System Call?
flashcards.back: An API is a general specification of how programs interact with software components (may be library-based). A System Call is a specific type of API that crosses the user-kernel boundary to request kernel services. All system calls are APIs, but not all APIs are system calls.

flashcards.front: List the major responsibilities of the Unix Kernel
flashcards.back: (1) Process scheduling, (2) Memory management, (3) File system operations, (4) Device I/O, (5) Inter-process communication, (6) Security and access control, (7) Networking.

flashcards.front: Why is C language preferred for Unix System Programming?
flashcards.back: C provides low-level access to memory through pointers, has minimal runtime overhead, produces efficient machine code, has direct access to system calls, and is the language in which Unix itself was originally written. It offers the right balance between portability and hardware access.

flashcards.front: What is the significance of file descriptors in Unix?
flashcards.back: A file descriptor is a small non-negative integer that serves as an index to a per-process file descriptor table entry. It represents an open file reference and is used by system calls like read(), write(), and close() to perform I/O operations.

flashcards.front: What are the three main categories of Unix system calls?
flashcards.back: (1) Process Control - fork(), exec(), wait(), exit(); (2) File Management - open(), read(), write(), close(); (3) Information Maintenance - getpid(), getuid(), uname(); (4) Communication - pipe(), socket(), msgget(); (5) Protection - chmod(), chown(), umask().
